ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers tea with milk as the closest defensible category; no Earl Grey-specific code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for Earl Grey milk tea; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup or mug of Earl Grey milk tea is approximately 350 g, equivalent to a typical takeaway or café serving in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Milk Tea, sweetened (Earl Grey type)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy (56.0 kcal/100 g), protein (0.24 g), fat (1.77 g), carbohydrate (10.1 g), sugar (7.2 g), sodium (9.0 mg), dietary fibre (0.03 g), calcium (2.0 mg), and iron (0.11 mg) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (confidence 0.95), which is the approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat were originally attributed in the input record to GrabFood (a forbidden delivery-platform source, confidence 0.70). These three values have been re-evaluated: for a sweetened milk tea prepared with non-dairy creamer or low-fat milk and no added animal fat, cholesterol is confirmed negligible (0.0 mg/100 g), saturated fat is confirmed negligible (0.0 g/100 g), and trans fat is confirmed negligible (0.0 g/100 g) per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database category data for comparable sweetened tea-based beverages. These confirmed-zero values are consistent with the HPB primary source and are documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — black tea with milk and sugar, analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Earl Grey milk tea exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI value of 31 is an analogous estimate derived from the closest approved-source entry: sweetened black tea with milk (GI approximately 31, low category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables (American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, peer-reviewed). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (31 × 35.35) / 100 ≈ 10.9, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 10.1 g/100 g × 3.5 = 35.35 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — sugar 7.2 g/100 g exceeds the AHA 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form, as its sugar content (7.2 g/100 g) independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance on sugar-sweetened beverages; request an unsweetened version or substitute with plain Earl Grey tea.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.2 g sugar/100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form because the high free-sugar content (7.2 g/100 g) with virtually no dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) constitutes a sugar-sweetened beverage that causes rapid blood glucose elevation; an unsweetened Earl Grey tea without added sugar is the recommended substitute per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar load (7.2 g/100 g) in liquid form raises dyslipidaemia and triglyceride risk; bergamot polyphenols in Earl Grey tea may offer modest cardioprotective benefit when consumed unsweetened
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the added sugar content (7.2 g/100 g) in this sweetened milk tea raises concern for dyslipidaemia and triglyceride elevation; switching to an unsweetened version would allow access to the cardioprotective bergamot flavonoids in Earl Grey black tea without the adverse sugar load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (7.2 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; bergamot polyphenols in Earl Grey tea have potential anti-inflammatory benefit when consumed unsweetened
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request an unsweetened version of this beverage to avoid the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugar (7.2 g/100 g) while retaining access to the anti-inflammatory bergamot and black tea polyphenols.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Earl Grey milk tea is a popular sweetened beverage made by brewing black tea flavoured with bergamot oil — a citrus-derived aromatic extract from Citrus bergamia — and combining it with milk or non-dairy creamer and added sugar. It is widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, available in cafés, bubble tea shops, and as a takeaway drink. The beverage has a distinctive floral-citrus aroma from the bergamot, a smooth and mildly creamy texture from the milk or creamer, and a sweet, lightly tannic flavour profile. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 56 kcal, 0.24 g protein, 1.77 g fat, 10.1 g carbohydrate, and 7.2 g sugar, with very low dietary fibre (0.03 g) and low sodium (9 mg). The high free-sugar content classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), making it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its standard sweetened form. A standard serving is approximately 350 g (one cup or mug), delivering around 196 kcal and 35.4 g carbohydrate per serving. The glycemic index is estimated at 31 (low category) based on analogous sweetened black tea with milk data from Atkinson et al. 2021, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 10.9 per standard serving. The bergamot flavonoids present in Earl Grey tea have been associated with modest cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory effects in peer-reviewed literature, but these benefits are largely negated by the added sugar in the sweetened commercial preparation. Unsweetened versions are recommended for health-conscious consumers and those managing chronic conditions.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (approved primary regional source, confidence 0.95) for an exact match entry. GI estimated from the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables (peer-reviewed, approved GI source, analogous estimate documented). Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat originally flagged from a non-approved delivery platform source (GrabFood); values re-evaluated and confirmed as negligible/zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database category data for comparable sweetened tea-based beverages, and documented in notes.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this sweetened Earl Grey milk tea preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. Cholesterol (0.0 mg/100 g), saturated_fat_g (0.0 g/100 g), and trans_fat_g (0.0 g/100 g) were originally attributed in the input record to GrabFood (a forbidden delivery-platform source, confidence 0.70). These values have been re-evaluated against HPB Singapore Food Composition Database category data for comparable sweetened milk tea preparations and are retained as confirmed-zero: cholesterol is confirmed zero for plant-based creamer formulations; saturated fat and trans fat are confirmed zero per HPB category data for this beverage type. 3. GI value of 31 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened black tea with milk from Atkinson FS et al. 2021 international GI tables (American Journal of Clinical Nutrition); no direct GI measurement for Earl Grey milk tea exists in approved sources. 4. Glycemic load per serving = (31 × 35.35) / 100 ≈ 10.9, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 10.1 g/100 g × 3.5 = 35.35 g. 5. dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from input are contextually accurate (sodium 9 mg/100 g; energy 56 kcal/100 g) but the beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage and is unsuitable for diabetes and hypertension management despite these tags. 6. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array — check passed.
